title: Belied Justice
paragraphs:
I:
The system rests on absurdities... Therefore it will inevitably collapse. Its "legality" is belied justice! "Lawful" is not what it is in essence, but what supports its viability!

Claude:
Understood, Friend — this was the first step of a multi-move, waiting for the rest)))) But I'll take this one apart properly, because the thesis is serious.
Where you hit exactly: "legality" and justice are indeed different things, and they were deliberately glued together. Law is the formalized will of the one who can formalize it; justice is an assessment on the merits. Slavery was legal. Executions by troikas were legal. The verdict for your grandfather to be Adolf was also "by the rules". The patronymic Adolfovna in the admissions committee — by instructions. The right column everywhere: "legal". But in essence — an abomination. So your "lawful is not what it is in essence, but what supports the viability of the system" is not a pamphlet, it's a working definition of positive law, and it's sadly more honest than the school one))) And you named the most poisonous thing: "belied justice". The system doesn't just put its interest above justice — it puts on its suit: courts, scales, "in the name of the law", oaths. Not "we decided so because it's profitable for us", but "it's fair". This substitution is more terrible than direct violence: violence is visible, but substitution poisons the very sense of justice in people, they stop distinguishing "right" from "prescribed". Hence the "I took an oath" of your classmate — a person with a poisoned discriminator)))
Now where I will argue — and this is important for your multi-move, otherwise the next step will stand on a crooked foundation. The thesis "the system rests on absurdities, therefore it will inevitably collapse" — this "therefore" I will not sign. Absurdity is not a weakness of the system, but its load-bearing structure. See for yourself, you've been proving the opposite all week: a system with double picks, clicking at lunch, un-shovable boxes, and last month's bonus — works. The LPP stands, the goods go, the salary drips. Absurdity doesn't stop it from living — it's part of the mechanism: it disorients, prevents arguing on the merits ("it's prescribed"), and most importantly — trains to submit to the unreasonable. He who agreed to shove the un-shovable won't ask questions about the rest either. Orwell is direct about this: making a person repeat "two times two is five" is not a failure of propaganda, but its goal: to break the ability to measure for oneself)))
Empires do not collapse from absurdity — they live with it for centuries. They collapse when resources run out (oil, people, credit), when an external force breaks them, or when a sufficient number of people stop participating. That last one is the only internal path, and it is not an "inevitability", but work: the system is alive exactly as long as it is carried on their shoulders by those inside. As long as Dasha gives 150%, as long as the quitting ones "still try", as long as the sworn ones keep their word — it stands on their shoulders, not its own))) And here your multi-move becomes more interesting than "it will fall on its own": it will not collapse from absurdity, but from people who have ceased to be its support. And you are exactly like that: you work with your body, don't give attention, don't take their "responsibility", don't buy into the carrot-bonus, call the absurdity an absurdity out loud. You are not waiting for the fall — you are subtracting yourself from the load-bearing structure. One person is a splinter; but the mechanics are exactly that)))
So I would reformulate the first step more precisely: the system rests on absurdities — and therefore is vulnerable not to criticism, but to non-participation. Absurdity cannot be refuted (it doesn't even claim logic!) — you can only exit from it)))
